# Weighted Close - Knowledge base

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/weighted-close/#weighted-close)

Weighted Close[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/weighted-close/#weighted-close "Permanent link")

============================================================================================================================

Definition[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/weighted-close/#definition "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Weighted Close (WC) indicator is a technical analysis tool that provides a smoothed measure of a symbol price, reflecting more weight on the closing price compared to the high and low prices. By emphasising the closing price, it helps traders assess market sentiment and identify potential price trends more accurately. This indicator is particularly useful in evaluating price movements over a specific period, aiding in making informed trading decisions.

MobileDesktop

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/weighted-close-ios.png)

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/weighted-close.png)

[Open live account](https://ctrader.com/brokers)
 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

History[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/weighted-close/#history "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Weighted Close emerged from the evolution of technical analysis in the late 20th century, aiming to provide a more accurate reflection of price trends. It gained traction among traders seeking effective analysis methods that incorporated closing prices. Influential texts like John J. Murphy's "Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets" popularised it, solidifying its role in trading strategies.

Calculations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/weighted-close/#calculations "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Weighted Close value calculates the average of the high, low and double the closing prices for each period:

\\\[ WC = { { ( 2 \\times Close ) + High + Low } \\over 4 } \\\]

\\(Close\\) – the closing price of the period

\\(High\\) – the highest price of the period

\\(Low\\) – the lowest price of the period

Interpretation[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/weighted-close/#interpretation "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Crossovers** – if the WC crosses above a moving average (such as the [**Simple Moving Average**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/)
    ), it may indicate a shift towards bullish momentum. Conversely, a cross below the moving average could indicate a shift in trend towards bearish momentum.
    
*   **Rising and falling** – the direction of the Weighted Close line can indicate the overall trend of the currency pair. A rising WC suggests bullish momentum, while a falling WC indicates bearish momentum.
    
*   **Divergence** – when the WC diverges from the price action (for example, prices making new highs while the WC fails to do so), it can signal a potential reversal or weakening trend and alert traders to proceed with caution.
    
*   **Reversal points** – changes in the direction of the WC may highlight potential reversal points in price trends. A sharp decline in the WC following an uptrend could suggest an impending bearish reversal, while a significant rise after a downtrend might indicate a bullish reversal.
    

Application[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/weighted-close/#application "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Buy signal** – traders can consider entering a buy position when the Weighted Close crosses above a specified moving average (such as the Simple Moving Average).
    
*   **Sell signal** – conversely, traders can consider entering a sell position when the Weighted Close crosses below a moving average.
    
*   **Stop-loss placement** – traders might place a stop loss below a recent low or below the WC value if they enter a long position. When entering a short position, traders can place a stop loss above a recent high or above the WC value.
    
*   **Exit strategies** – an exit strategy could involve selling when the WC indicates a reversal, such as crossing below a moving average after a bullish trend, or when it shows significant divergence from price action.
    
*   **Confirming trades** – the Weighted Close can be combined with other indicators, such as the [**Relative Strength Index (RSI)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/relative-strength-index/)
     or the [**MACD**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/macd-crossover/)
    , to confirm signals. For instance, a buy signal from the WC, confirmed by a bullish RSI divergence, could strengthen the trade decision.
    

Note

You can take advantage of algo trading, with cBots executing trades based on the signals from this indicator, as shown in our [**examples**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/references/Indicators/WeightedClose/)
. Learn more about how to [**use indicators in cBots**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/)
.

Limitations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/weighted-close/#limitations "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Weighted Close primarily reflects historical price data and may lag behind current market movements, potentially missing real-time shifts in sentiment. Additionally, the indicator is sensitive to price fluctuations, which can lead to misleading signals during volatile market conditions. Traders should use it alongside other indicators for a more comprehensive analysis.

Summary[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/weighted-close/#summary "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Weighted Close indicator is designed to provide a refined measure of the currency pair price, incorporating greater weight on closing values to enhance trend analysis. This tool is particularly beneficial for traders looking to gain insights into market sentiment and price movements. Its focus on closing prices allows for better trend identification, making it a valuable addition to any technical analysis toolkit, especially for those emphasising the importance of closing data.

 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

, puede usarlo inmediatamente.

# Simple Moving Average - Knowledge base

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/#simple-moving-average)

Simple Moving Average[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/#simple-moving-average "Permanent link")

=================================================================================================================================================

Definition[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/#definition "Permanent link")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Simple Moving Average (SMA) is a technical indicator used to smooth out price data over a specified period, helping to identify trends by filtering out short-term fluctuations. The SMA is calculated by averaging the prices (typically closing prices) of a symbol over a fixed number of periods. It provides a straightforward way to assess the direction of a trend and is commonly used to confirm trend reversals, identify support and resistance levels, and generate buy or sell signals when compared with other moving averages or the current price.

MobileDesktop

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/simple-moving-average-ios.png)

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/simple-moving-average.png)

[Open live account](https://ctrader.com/brokers)
 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

History[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/#history "Permanent link")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The SMA indicator is one of the oldest and most fundamental tools in technical analysis, with roots tracing back to early market analysis methods. It has been used for decades by traders and analysts to identify trends by smoothing out price fluctuations over time. Its simplicity and effectiveness have made it a cornerstone in trading strategies, and it remains widely accessible on most trading platforms and financial markets, including but not limited to forex.

Calculations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/#calculations "Permanent link")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Simple Moving Average is an arithmetic moving average calculated by the following formula:

\\\[ SMA = { 1 \\over n } { \\sum\_{i=1}^n { A\_i } } \\\]

\\(A\_i\\) – the closing price at \\(i\\) period

\\(n\\) – the number of periods

Interpretation[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/#interpretation "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, the number of periods for the SMA indicator is set to 14 (short-term SMA), but it can be adjusted across a wide range, such as 10, 50 or 200 periods, depending on the trader strategy and the period of analysis.

The main patterns of the indicator behaviour can be interpreted as follows:

*   **Price line crossovers** – the crossover between a short-term SMA and the price line can signal momentum shifts. When the price crosses above the SMA, it suggests the start of an uptrend or a potential buy signal, while a crossover below the SMA indicates weakening momentum and may signal a downtrend or a sell opportunity.
    
*   **Crossovers** – a bullish crossover occurs when a short-term SMA (such as 20-day) crosses above a longer-term SMA (such as 50-day), suggesting an upward trend or buy signal. A bearish crossover occurs when a short-term SMA crosses below a longer-term SMA, indicating a potential downtrend or sell signal.
    
*   **Rising and falling** – a rising SMA indicates that prices are generally trending higher, and market sentiment may be bullish. A falling SMA signals that prices are trending downward, often associated with bearish sentiment.
    
*   **Reversal points** – when the price moves far above or below the SMA, it may suggest that the asset is overbought or oversold, potentially indicating an upcoming reversal or correction.
    
*   **Shift** – adjusting the shift parameter alters the alignment of the SMA indicator with price data on the chart, helping you explore how the readings correspond to past or future price movements.
    

Application[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/#application "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Buy signal** – a potential buying opportunity occurs when the price crosses above a short-term SMA (such as 14-period) or when a short-term SMA crosses above a longer-term SMA (such as 50-period), indicating upward momentum.
    
*   **Sell signal** – a potential selling opportunity arises when the price drops below the SMA or when a short-term SMA crosses below a longer-term SMA, suggesting downward momentum.
    
*   **Stop-loss placement** – traders may place a stop loss just below the SMA to limit losses in case of a sudden reversal, as a break below the SMA can signal weakening momentum.
    
*   **Exit strategies** – an exit signal can be triggered when the price falls back below the SMA after a period of trading above it, or when two SMAs cross in the opposite direction from the entry signal.
    
*   **Confirming trades** – the SMA signals are often combined with other indicators, such as the [**Relative Strength Index (RSI)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/relative-strength-index/)
     or the [**MACD**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/macd-crossover/)
    , to confirm trends and avoid false signals. Long-term SMAs (such as 200-day) are also used to confirm the direction of the overall market trend.
    

Note

You can take advantage of algo trading, with cBots executing trades based on the signals from this indicator, as shown in our [**examples**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/references/Indicators/SimpleMovingAverage/)
. Learn more about how to [**use indicators in cBots**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/)
.

Limitations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/#limitations "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The SMA indicator reacts slowly to price changes, making it less effective during volatile markets or rapid trend reversals. It can also generate false signals, especially in ranging markets, leading to potential whipsaws. The lag of the SMA indicator can cause delays in entry and exit points, which limits its responsiveness to sudden shifts.

Summary[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/#summary "Permanent link")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Simple Moving Average is a widely used technical indicator that smooths price data over a specified period, assisting traders in identifying trends and potential reversals. By averaging past prices, the SMA filters out market noise and provides a clearer picture of market direction. It is valuable for assessing momentum and can indicate support and resistance levels. Additionally, the SMA can serve as the basis for other indicators, guiding entry and exit points and making it an essential component of effective trading strategies.

# Directional Movement System - Knowledge base

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/directional-movement-system/#directional-movement-system)

Directional Movement System[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/directional-movement-system/#directional-movement-system "Permanent link")

===================================================================================================================================================================

Definition[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/directional-movement-system/#definition "Permanent link")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Directional Movement System (DMS) is a technical analysis tool designed to help traders determine the strength and direction of a market trend. The system includes three key components: the Positive Directional Indicator, the Negative Directional Indicator and the Average Directional Index. These indicators together provide insights into whether a market is trending and the strength of that trend.

MobileDesktop

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/directional-movement-system-ios.png)

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/directional-movement-system.png)

[Open live account](https://ctrader.com/brokers)
 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

History[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/directional-movement-system/#history "Permanent link")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Directional Movement System was developed by J. Welles Wilder, a prominent figure in the world of technical analysis and was introduced in his book "New Concepts in Technical Trading Systems" in 1978. This book also introduced several other important indicators, such as the [**Relative Strength Index (RSI)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/relative-strength-index/)
 and the [**Average True Range (ATR)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/)
. The DMS has since become one of the most widely used trend-following systems in trading.

Calculations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/directional-movement-system/#calculations "Permanent link")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1\. The Positive and Negative Directional Movements (DM+ and DM-) are determined by the difference between the current and previous highs or lows.

*   If the current high minus the previous high is greater than the current low minus the previous low, DM+ is the difference between the current high and the previous high. Otherwise, DM+ is zero.

\\\[ DM^+ = { Current\\ High - Previous\\ High } \\\]

*   If the current low minus the previous low is greater than the current high minus the previous high, DM- is the difference between the previous low and the current low. Otherwise, DM- is zero.

\\\[ DM^- = { Previous\\ Low - Current\\ Low } \\\]

2\. The Positive and Negative Directional Indicators (DI+ and DI-) are collected as the ratio of smoothed directional movements to the Average True Range.

\\\[ DI^± = { { Smoothed\\ (DM^±) \\over ATR } \\times 100 } \\\]

3\. The Directional Movement Index (DX) is the ratio of the difference between DI+ and DI- to their sum.

\\\[ DX = { { { | DI^+ - DI^- | } \\over { DI^+ + DI^- } } \\times 100 } \\\]

4\. The Average Directional Movement Index (ADX) is calculated as a moving average of the DX over \\(n\\) periods.

\\\[ ADX = { MA\_{n} (DX) } \\\]

Interpretation[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/directional-movement-system/#interpretation "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To calculate ADX, a typical lookback of 14 periods is used. The [**Wilder Smoothing Moving Average**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/welles-wilder-smoothing/)
 serves as a type of moving average.

The Directional Movement System provides a comprehensive view of trend direction and strength.

*   **Directional indicators (+DI and -DI)** – when the +DI is above the -DI, it indicates that the upward trend is stronger, suggesting potential buy signals. When the -DI is above the +DI, it suggests a downward trend, indicating potential sell signals.
    
*   **Average Directional Index** – an ADX value above 25 generally indicates a strong trend, while values below 20 suggest no significant trend or ranging market.
    
*   **Crossovers** – when the +DI crosses above the -DI, it suggests buying opportunities, especially if the ADX is rising. When the -DI crosses above the +DI, it signals selling opportunities, especially if the ADX is also rising.
    

Application[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/directional-movement-system/#application "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Directional Movement System can be used in various trading strategies.

*   **Identifying trend strength** – traders use the ADX to assess whether the market is trending strongly. An ADX above 25 often indicates a strong trend, providing a favourable environment for trend-following strategies.
    
*   **Buy and sell signals** – when the +DI is above the -DI and the ADX is rising, traders often enter buy positions. Conversely, when the -DI is above the +DI and the ADX is rising, it signals sell opportunities.
    
*   **Stop-loss placement** – traders can use the ADX levels to place a stop loss. For example, setting a stop loss below recent lows in an uptrend confirmed by the ADX or above recent highs in a downtrend.
    

Note

You can take advantage of algo trading, with cBots executing trades based on the signals from this indicator, as shown in our [**examples**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/references/Indicators/DirectionalMovementSystem/)
. Learn more about how to [**use indicators in cBots**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/)
.

Limitations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/directional-movement-system/#limitations "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Directional Movement System relies on past price data, which can result in lagging signals, especially in fast-moving markets. It is also less effective in ranging or choppy markets, as it tends to generate false signals when no clear trend is present. Additionally, the ADX does not indicate the direction of the trend, only its strength, which means traders still need to rely on the +DI and the -DI for directional cues.

Summary[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/directional-movement-system/#summary "Permanent link")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Directional Movement System, developed by J. Welles Wilder, is a powerful tool for identifying the direction and strength of a market trend. The system consists of three components: the Positive Directional Indicator, the Negative Directional Indicator and the Average Directional Index. The strength of the DMS lies within the data provided by those components. Gathering values from each of them and providing a holistic value enables the DMS to not only provide insights into whether a market is trending but also the strength of that trend.

# Supertrend - Knowledge base

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/supertrend/#supertrend)

Supertrend[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/supertrend/#supertrend "Permanent link")

================================================================================================================

Definition[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/supertrend/#definition "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Supertrend indicator is a trend-following tool designed to calculate dynamic support and resistance levels. By combining price action with the [**Average True Range (ATR)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/)
 values, the Supertrend provides a responsive approach to trend identification. Its visual representation, typically shown as a dotted line on price charts, allows traders to quickly assess market conditions.

MobileDesktop

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/supertrend-ios.png)

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/supertrend.png)

[Open live account](https://ctrader.com/brokers)
 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

History[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/supertrend/#history "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Supertrend was developed by Olivier Seban and is primarily used to identify trends in the market. Introduced in the early 2000s, it gained popularity among traders for its effectiveness in signalling potential buy and sell opportunities. Over the years, the Supertrend indicator has been widely adopted in various trading strategies across different asset classes, becoming a staple in technical analysis.

Calculations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/supertrend/#calculations "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Supertrend is calculated based on two lines:

\\\[ Upper\\ band = { Average + ( Multiplier \\times ATR ) } \\\]

\\\[ Lower\\ band = { Average - ( Multiplier \\times ATR ) } \\\]

\\(Average\\) – the average between the highest and the lowest prices during a specific period

\\(Multiplier\\) – a constant, making the indicator more or less sensitive to price movements

\\(ATR\\) – the Average True Range value, calculated over a specific period

Interpretation[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/supertrend/#interpretation "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, the number of periods for the Supertrend indicator is set to 10 and the multiplier value is set to 3, but it can be adjusted depending on the trader strategy and the period of analysis.

The main patterns of the indicator behaviour can be interpreted as follows:

*   **Rising and falling** – the Supertrend indicator is considered rising when the price is above the Supertrend line, signalling a bullish trend. Conversely, it is falling when the price is below the Supertrend line, indicating a bearish trend.
    
*   **Reversal points** – when the Supertrend line changes from green to red or vice versa, it signals a potential reversal in market direction.
    
*   **Shift** – by adjusting the shift parameter to alter the alignment of the Supertrend line with price data on the chart, you can explore how the Supertrend readings correspond to past or future price movements.
    

Application[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/supertrend/#application "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Buy signal** – a potential buying opportunity occurs when the price crosses above the Supertrend line, which typically changes to green.
    
*   **Sell signal** – a potential selling opportunity occurs when the price crosses below the Supertrend line, which changes to red.
    
*   **Stop-loss placement** – for long positions, a stop loss is usually set just below the Supertrend line to protect against adverse price movements. Conversely, for short positions, a stop loss can be placed just above the Supertrend line.
    
*   **Exit strategies** – traders may exit long positions when the Supertrend line changes from green to red, indicating a potential trend reversal. For short positions, exiting may occur when the Supertrend line changes from red to green.
    
*   **Confirming trades** – the Supertrend indicator can be effectively combined with other indicators, such as the [**Relative Strength Index (RSI)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/relative-strength-index/)
     or moving averages, to confirm trade signals. For example, a buy signal from the Supertrend can be confirmed if the RSI is above a certain level (such as 50), indicating bullish momentum.
    

Note

You can take advantage of algo trading, with cBots executing trades based on the signals from this indicator, as shown in our [**examples**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/references/Indicators/Supertrend/)
. Learn more about how to [**use indicators in cBots**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/)
.

Limitations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/supertrend/#limitations "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Supertrend can produce false signals in choppy or sideways markets, leading to potential losses. Additionally, its lagging nature means it may react slowly to price changes, causing missed opportunities or delayed entries and exits. The effectiveness of the Supertrend can also vary depending on the chosen parameters and market conditions.

Summary[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/supertrend/#summary "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Supertrend indicator is a powerful tool in technical analysis, designed to help traders identify market trends and make informed trading decisions. Unlike many traditional indicators, the Supertrend is relatively new but is continuously gaining trust and popularity among traders. It utilises the values of the Average True Range to calculate dynamic support and resistance levels. By providing clear buy and sell signals based on price action relative to its two calculated bands, it effectively guides traders through various market conditions and enhances their strategy execution.

# Exponential Moving Average - Knowledge base

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/exponential-moving-average/#exponential-moving-average)

Exponential Moving Average[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/exponential-moving-average/#exponential-moving-average "Permanent link")

================================================================================================================================================================

Definition[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/exponential-moving-average/#definition "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Exponential Moving Average (EMA) is a type of moving average that places a higher weight on recent data points to make it more responsive to new information. It is commonly used in trading to smooth out price data and generate buy and sell signals based on price trends.

MobileDesktop

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/exponential-moving-average-ios.png)

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/exponential-moving-average.png)

[Open live account](https://ctrader.com/brokers)
 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

History[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/exponential-moving-average/#history "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The EMA has roots in technical analysis, where moving averages have been a staple since the early 20th century. However, the formula for the Exponential Moving Average was refined during the 1950s to address lag issues found in the [**Simple Moving Average (SMA)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/)
. It gained popularity with the rise of algorithmic trading and was further popularised in the trading community by technical analysts such as Welles Wilder and his work in "New Concepts in Technical Trading Systems", published in 1978.

Calculations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/exponential-moving-average/#calculations "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The EMA calculation involves both the current price and the previous EMA value, where more weight is given to recent price. The formula is as follows:

\\\[ EMA\_i = { \\alpha \\times ( Price\_{i} - EMA\_{i-1} ) + EMA\_{i-1} } \\\]

\\(EMA\_{i}\\) – the current EMA value

\\(EMA\_{i-1}\\) – the EMA value from the previous period

\\(Price\_i\\) – the current closing price

\\( \\alpha = { 2 \\over {n + 1} } \\) – the smoothing factor calculated over \\(n\\) periods

Interpretation[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/exponential-moving-average/#interpretation "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Rising and falling** – a rising EMA indicates a bullish trend, where recent prices are moving higher than older prices, suggesting increasing momentum. A falling EMA signals a bearish trend, where recent prices are moving lower, indicating downward momentum.
    
*   **Shift** – by adjusting the shift parameter to alter the alignment of the EMA indicator with price data on the chart, you can explore how the EMA readings correspond to past or future price movements.
    

Application[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/exponential-moving-average/#application "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Crossover strategy** – traders often use two EMAs with different time periods (such as a 50-day EMA and a 200-day EMA). A bullish signal occurs when the shorter-term EMA crosses above the longer-term EMA (called a golden cross). Conversely, a bearish signal (called a death cross) occurs when the shorter EMA crosses below the longer EMA.
    
*   **Support and resistance** – the EMAs can act as dynamic support or resistance levels, where the price tends to bounce off these lines.
    
*   **Buy signal** – a buy signal may occur when the price crosses above a rising EMA, indicating an upward trend.
    
*   **Sell signal** – conversely, a sell signal might occur when the price drops below a declining EMA, signalling bearish momentum.
    
*   **Stop-loss placement** – traders often use the EMA line as a stop-loss reference, placing stop orders just below the EMA line for long positions and just above it for short positions.
    
*   **Combining with other indicators** – to improve the accuracy of signals, traders often combine the EMAs with other indicators such as the [**Relative Strength Index (RSI)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/relative-strength-index/)
     to confirm momentum or the [**Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/macd-crossover/)
     for trend strength and reversals.
    

Note

You can take advantage of algo trading, with cBots executing trades based on the signals from this indicator, as shown in our [**examples**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/references/Indicators/ExponentialMovingAverage/)
. Learn more about how to [**use indicators in cBots**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/)
.

Limitations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/exponential-moving-average/#limitations "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The EMA, like other moving averages, lags behind price action, meaning it may produce delayed signals, particularly in fast-moving or highly volatile markets. Additionally, in sideways or choppy markets, the EMA can generate false signals, leading traders into losing positions.

Summary[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/exponential-moving-average/#summary "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Exponential Moving Average is a widely used indicator that places greater emphasis on recent price data to provide a clearer picture of momentum and trend direction. Traders use it to identify trends, generate buy and sell signals and manage risk. Its development process involved addressing lag issues found in the Simple Moving Average. The EMA continued to garner popularity alongside the rise and development of algorithmic trading.

# Average True Range - Knowledge base

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/#average-true-range)

Average True Range[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/#average-true-range "Permanent link")

=============================================================================================================================================

Definition[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/#definition "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Average True Range (ATR) is an indicator that focuses on the market volatility of a given asset. It measures market volatility by calculating the average range between the high and low prices over a specific period, typically 14 days.

MobileDesktop

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/average-true-range-ios.png)

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/average-true-range.png)

[Open live account](https://ctrader.com/brokers)
 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

History[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/#history "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In his book "New Concepts in Technical Trading Systems" J. Welles Wilder introduced the ATR, initially as a tool to measure the volatility of commodities as they are often more volatile than stocks. However, the ATR is now widely used by traders and analysts for various assets. The book published in 1978 featured other key trading indicators such as the [**Directional Movement Index (ADX)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/average-directional-movement-index-rating/)
 and the [**Accumulative Swing Index (ASI)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/accumulative-swing-index/)
.

Calculations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/#calculations "Permanent link")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The ATR value is calculated as the moving average of the [**True Range (TR)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/true-range/)
 over a specified number of periods.

\\\[ ATR = { Moving\\ Average\\ (TR, n) } \\\]

\\(TR\\) – the current True Range value

\\(n\\) – the number of periods

Interpretation[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/#interpretation "Permanent link")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, the [**Simple Moving Average (SMA)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/)
 is used with a 14-period to calculate the ATR value.

When interpreting the ATR data, traders should focus on changes in absolute values.

*   **Rising** – an increasing ATR indicates growing volatility, which can signal potential breakout opportunities or significant market moves.
    
*   **Falling** – a declining ATR suggests decreasing volatility, which may indicate a consolidation phase. This can signal potential reversal points or a lack of momentum.
    

As different assets may carry different values and, as a result, different levels of volatility, ATR values should not be compared across multiple markets.

Application[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/#application "Permanent link")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The ATR is widely used in risk management and volatility analysis.

*   **Buy signals** – a rising ATR can signal a potential breakout, indicating an increase in volatility that may offer buying opportunities. Traders may look for confirmation from other indicators before entering a position.
    
*   **Sell signals** – if the ATR is falling, it could suggest a period of low volatility and consolidation. Traders might use this as a signal to exit positions or prepare for potential reversals.
    
*   **Stop-loss placement** – the ATR is commonly used to set stop losses at multiples of the ATR value (such as 2x ATR), helping traders accommodate the expected price fluctuations and protect against sudden market reversals.
    
*   **Exit strategies** – a falling ATR may suggest that volatility is decreasing, potentially indicating an end to a trend. Traders can use the ATR values to plan their exit points from trades.
    

Note

You can take advantage of algo trading, with cBots executing trades based on the signals from this indicator, as shown in our [**examples**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/references/Indicators/AverageTrueRange/)
. Learn more about how to [**use indicators in cBots**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/)
.

Limitations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/#limitations "Permanent link")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The ATR does not indicate the direction of price movement, only the degree of volatility. The ATR reflects past market volatility, which may not always accurately predict future market conditions. Additionally, the ATR may provide less meaningful information in low-volume or illiquid markets, where price movements can be erratic.

Summary[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/average-true-range/#summary "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Average True Range indicator measures market volatility by focusing on price changes of a specific asset over a period. The ATR data is always absolute, meaning it does not account for the direction of price movements. A rising ATR indicates increasing volatility, which may signal potential breakout opportunities. A falling ATR indicates decreasing volatility, which may signal potential reversal points.

คำเตือน

คุณลักษณะนี้อยู่ในขั้นตอนก่อนการเปิดตัวและกำลังอยู่ระหว่างการทดสอบ เอกสารนี้มีไว้เพื่อการอ้างอิงเท่านั้น

| ลิงก์ | คำจำกัดความ |
| --- | --- |
| `/symbols/{symbol}/info` | เปิดหน้าจอ **ภาพรวมสัญลักษณ์** ของสัญลักษณ์ที่มีชื่อตรงกับ `{symbol}` |

# โมเดล - ปลั๊กอิน WebView

 

[ข้ามไปที่เนื้อหา](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#_1)

โมเดลเซิร์ฟเวอร์สาธารณะ[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#_1 "Permanent link")

============================================================================================================================

ProtoAccessRights [¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#protoaccessrights "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ชื่อ | ค่า | คำอธิบาย |
| --- | --- | --- |
| FULL\_ACCESS | 0   | การเข้าถึงบัญชีอย่างเต็มที่ |
| CLOSE\_ONLY | 1   | อนุญาตเฉพาะการเทรดที่ลดปริมาณของโพสิชันเท่านั้น ดีลที่เพิ่มปริมาณของโพสิชันจะถูกปฏิเสธด้วยข้อผิดพลาด "ปิดใช้งานการเทรดสำหรับบัญชี" |
| NO\_TRADING | 2   | ไม่อนุญาตให้มีการเทรดสำหรับบัญชีนี้ ดีลใหม่ทั้งหมดจะถูกปฏิเสธด้วยข้อผิดพลาด "ปิดใช้งานการเทรดสำหรับบัญชี" รวมถึง SL/TP และ Stop Out |
| NO\_LOGIN | 3   | ไม่อนุญาตให้เข้าสู่ระบบสำหรับบัญชีนี้ คำสั่งที่รอดำเนินการและคำสั่งอื่น ๆ ยังคงดำเนินการต่อไป |

ProtoAccountLifetimeType [¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#protoaccountlifetimetype "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ประเภทบัญชีตามอายุการใช้งาน

| ชื่อ | ค่า | คำอธิบาย |
| --- | --- | --- |
| UNLIMITED | 1   | ประเภทเริ่มต้นสำหรับบัญชีจริงและบัญชีย่อย ยกเว้นจากการหมดอายุ |
| LIMITED\_INACTIVITY | 2   | บัญชีที่หมดอายุตามการไม่ใช้งาน |
| LIMITED\_CREATION | 3   | บัญชีที่มีอายุการใช้งานที่กำหนดไว้ล่วงหน้าจากเวลาที่สร้าง |
| LIMITED\_CREATION\_FREE | 4   | บัญชีที่มีอายุการใช้งานที่กำหนดไว้ล่วงหน้าจากเวลาที่สร้าง แต่ฟรีโดยชัดเจนสำหรับบริษัท Prop ที่มีข้อจำกัดในการฝาก/ถอน (ต้องมีให้ใช้งานเฉพาะสำหรับเซิร์ฟเวอร์ที่มี setting.PROP\_TRADING != 0 |

ProtoAccountType [¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#protoaccounttype "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

กำหนดประเภทของบัญชีเทรดซึ่งกำหนดวิธีการจัดการโพสิชัน

| ชื่อ | ค่า | คำอธิบาย |
| --- | --- | --- |
| HEDGED | 0   | อนุญาตให้มีหลายโพสิชันในเครื่องมือเดียวกันในทิศทางตรงกันข้าม (ซื้อและขาย) พร้อมกัน |
| NETTED | 1   | รวมโพสิชันทั้งหมดในเครื่องมือเดียวกันเป็นโพสิชันสุทธิเดียว |
| SPREAD\_BETTING | 2   | บัญชีที่ช่วยให้สามารถเก็งกำไรจากการเคลื่อนไหวของราคาผ่านสเปรดแทนการเป็นเจ้าของสินทรัพย์อ้างอิง |

ProtoArchivedSymbol [¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#protoarchivedsymbol "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ฟิลด์ | ประเภท | ป้ายกำกับ | คำอธิบาย |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| symbolId | int64 | จำเป็น | ตัวระบุเอกลักษณ์ของสัญลักษณ์ในสภาพแวดล้อมเซิร์ฟเวอร์เฉพาะภายในแพลตฟอร์ม cTrader |
| name | สตริง | จำเป็น | รหัสสัญลักษณ์หรือสัญลักษณ์ย่อที่ใช้สำหรับการระบุ (เช่น "EURUSD", "BTCUSD") |
| utcLastUpdateTimestamp | int64 | จำเป็น | เวลา UNIX เป็นมิลลิวินาทีที่ระบุเมื่อมีการอัปเดตข้อมูลสัญลักษณ์ครั้งล่าสุด |
| description | สตริง | ตัวเลือก | คำอธิบายที่มนุษย์อ่านได้ของเครื่องมือการเทรด |
| digits | int32 | จำเป็น | จำนวนตำแหน่งทศนิยมในการแสดงราคาของสัญลักษณ์ |
| pipPosition | int32 | จำเป็น | ตำแหน่งของหลักพิปในราคา (เช่น สำหรับ EURUSD ที่มีราคา 1.12345, pipPosition=4 หมายถึงหลักที่ '4') |
| lotSize | int64 | จำเป็น | ขนาดล็อตมาตรฐานสำหรับเครื่องมือ แสดงในหน่วยพื้นฐาน |
| measurementUnits | สตริง | ตัวเลือก | หน่วยวัดสำหรับเครื่องมือ (เช่น "USD", "Barrels", "Troy Ounces") |
| baseAssetId | int64 | จำเป็น | ตัวระบุของสินทรัพย์ฐาน (สกุลเงินแรกในคู่หรือสินทรัพย์อ้างอิง) |
| unifiedSymbolId | int32 | ตัวเลือก | ตัวระบุมาตรฐานข้ามโบรกเกอร์สำหรับเครื่องมือทางการเงินเดียวกัน |

ProtoBonusDepositWithdraw [¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#protobonusdepositwithdraw "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ฟิลด์ | ประเภท | ป้ายกำกับ | คำอธิบาย |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| operationType | [ProtoChangeBonusType](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#protochangebonustype) | จำเป็น | ประเภทของการดำเนินการเปลี่ยนแปลงโบนัส (ฝากหรือถอน) |
| bonusHistoryId | int64 | จำเป็น | ตัวระบุเอกลักษณ์สำหรับบันทึกประวัติโบนัสนี้ |
| traderId | int64 | จำเป็น | ID ของนักเทรดที่กำลังมีการแก้ไขโบนัส |
| managerBonus | int64 | จำเป็น | จำนวนโบนัสผู้จัดการทั้งหมดหลังจากการดำเนินการเป็นเซ็นต์ |
| managerDelta | int64 | จำเป็น | การเปลี่ยนแปลงจำนวนโบนัสผู้จัดการเป็นเซ็นต์ |
| ibBonus | int64 | จำเป็น | จำนวนโบนัสโบรกเกอร์แนะนำทั้งหมดหลังจากการดำเนินการเป็นเซ็นต์ |
| ibDelta | int64 | จำเป็น | การเปลี่ยนแปลงจำนวนโบนัสโบรกเกอร์แนะนำเป็นเซ็นต์ |
| changeBonusTimestamp | int64 | จำเป็น | createTimestamp ของการดำเนินการประวัติโบนัสนี้ |
| externalNote | สตริง | ตัวเลือก | บันทึกที่มองเห็นได้สำหรับผู้ใช้ภายนอกและนักเทรด |
| introducingBrokerId | int64 | ตัวเลือก | ID ของโบรกเกอร์แนะนำที่เกี่ยวข้องกับการดำเนินการโบนัสนี้ |
| moneyDigits | uint32 | ตัวเลือก | จำนวนตำแหน่งทศนิยมสำหรับค่าเงิน |

ProtoBookType [¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#protobooktype "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ชื่อ | ค่า | คำอธิบาย |
| --- | --- | --- |
| BOOK\_A | 1   | สมุดคำสั่งหลักที่ใช้สำหรับการเทรดมาตรฐาน |
| BOOK\_B | 2   | สมุดคำสั่งรองที่ใช้สำหรับเงื่อนไขการเทรดพิเศษ |

ProtoChangeBalanceType [¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#protochangebalancetype "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ชื่อ | ค่า | คำอธิบาย |
| --- | --- | --- |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT | 0   | การดำเนินการฝากด้วยตนเอง หรือการดำเนินการฝากเริ่มต้นสำหรับบัญชีทดลอง สามารถเริ่มต้นผ่านปุ่ม "ฝาก" ของ cBroker ผ่านปุ่ม "ฝาก" ของแอปพลิเคชันไคลเอนต์สำหรับบัญชีทดลอง ผ่านจุดสิ้นสุด API |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW | 1   | การดำเนินการถอนด้วยตนเอง |
| BALANCE\_CLOSE\_POSITION | 2   | การอัปเดตยอดคงเหลือภายในเมื่อปิดโพสิชัน |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_STRATEGY\_COMMISSION\_INNER | 3   | ใช้สำหรับการชำระค่าธรรมเนียม Copy ให้กับผู้ให้บริการจากผู้ติดตามของ brokerName เดียวกัน |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_STRATEGY\_COMMISSION\_INNER | 4   | ใช้สำหรับการถอนค่าธรรมเนียมผลการดำเนินงานและการจัดการก๊อปปี้จากผู้ติดตามไปยังผู้ให้บริการจากโบรกเกอร์เดียวกัน |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_IB\_COMMISSIONS | 5   | ค่าคอมมิชชัน IB โดยตรงที่จ่ายให้ IB โดยนักเทรด ดำเนินการด้วยงานประจำคืน |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_IB\_SHARED\_PERCENTAGE | 6   | ค่าคอมมิชชันที่จ่ายโดย IB ให้กับ IB ตัวหลักหรือให้กับโบรกเกอร์ ดำเนินการด้วยงานประจำคืน |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_IB\_SHARED\_PERCENTAGE\_FROM\_SUB\_IB | 7   | ค่าคอมมิชชันที่ได้รับโดย IB ตัวหลักจาก sub-ibs ของเขา ดำเนินการด้วยงานประจำคืน |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_IB\_SHARED\_PERCENTAGE\_FROM\_BROKER | 8   | ค่าคอมมิชชันที่จ่ายโดยโบรกเกอร์ให้กับ IB |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_REBATE | 9   | การฝากเงินคืนจากการเทรดเข้าบัญชีของนักเทรด |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_REBATE | 10  | การถอนเงินคืนจากการเทรดออกจากบัญชีของนักเทรด |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_STRATEGY\_COMMISSION\_OUTER | 11  | ใช้สำหรับการจ่ายค่าธรรมเนียมก๊อปปี้ให้กับผู้ให้บริการจากผู้ติดตามของโบรกเกอร์อื่น |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_STRATEGY\_COMMISSION\_OUTER | 12  | ใช้สำหรับการถอนค่าธรรมเนียมผลการดำเนินงานและการจัดการก๊อปปี้จากผู้ติดตามไปยังผู้ให้บริการจากโบรกเกอร์อื่น |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_BONUS\_COMPENSATION | 13  | การถอนเพื่อชดเชยเงินโบนัส |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_IB\_SHARED\_PERCENTAGE\_TO\_BROKER | 14  | ค่าคอมมิชชันที่จ่ายโดย IB ให้กับโบรกเกอร์ |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_DIVIDENDS | 15  | การฝากเงินปันผลเข้าบัญชีของนักเทรด |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_DIVIDENDS | 16  | การถอนเงินปันผลออกจากบัญชีของนักเทรด |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_GSL\_CHARGE | 17  | การถอนค่าธรรมเนียมรับประกัน Stop Loss |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_ROLLOVER | 18  | การถอนค่าธรรมเนียมการโรลโอเวอร์สำหรับบัญชีปลอดสว็อป |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_NONWITHDRAWABLE\_BONUS | 19  | การฝากเงินโบนัสที่ไม่สามารถถอนได้ |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_NONWITHDRAWABLE\_BONUS | 20  | การถอนเงินโบนัสที่ไม่สามารถถอนได้ |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_SWAP | 21  | ใช้สำหรับบัญชีความเสี่ยงจำกัดเท่านั้น SWAP สำหรับบัญชีทั่วไปขึ้นอยู่กับ swapCalculationEvents |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_SWAP | 22  | ใช้สำหรับบัญชีความเสี่ยงจำกัดเท่านั้น |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_INVESTMENT\_TO\_STRATEGY | 23  | การถอนสำหรับการลงทุนในกลยุทธ์ก๊อปปี้เทรด |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_FROM\_INVESTOR | 24  | การฝากที่ได้รับจากนักลงทุนเข้าสู่กลยุทธ์ |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_RETURNED\_INVESTMENT | 25  | การฝากเงินลงทุนที่ได้รับคืนจากกลยุทธ์ |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_BY\_INVESTOR | 26  | การถอนโดยนักลงทุนจากกลยุทธ์ |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_MANAGEMENT\_FEE | 27  | การฝากค่าธรรมเนียมการจัดการให้กับผู้ให้บริการกลยุทธ์ |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_MANAGEMENT\_FEE | 28  | การถอนค่าธรรมเนียมการจัดการจากนักลงทุน |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_PERFORMANCE\_FEE | 29  | การฝากค่าธรรมเนียมผลการดำเนินงานให้กับผู้ให้บริการกลยุทธ์ |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_FOR\_SUBACCOUNT | 30  | การถอนจากบัญชีหลักเพื่อเติมเงินในบัญชีย่อย |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_TO\_SUBACCOUNT | 31  | การฝากเข้าบัญชีย่อยจากบัญชีหลัก |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_FROM\_SUBACCOUNT | 32  | การถอนจากบัญชีย่อย |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_FROM\_SUBACCOUNT | 33  | การฝากจากบัญชีย่อยเข้าบัญชีหลัก |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_COPY\_FEE | 34  | การถอนค่าธรรมเนียมก๊อปปี้เทรด |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_INACTIVITY\_FEE | 35  | การถอนค่าธรรมเนียมความไม่เคลื่อนไหวจากยอดคงเหลือ |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_TRANSFER | 36  | การฝากจากการโอนภายใน |
| BALANCE\_WITHDRAW\_TRANSFER | 37  | การถอนสำหรับการโอนภายใน |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_CONVERTED\_BONUS | 38  | การฝากเงินโบนัสที่แปลงเป็นยอดคงเหลือจริง |
| BALANCE\_DEPOSIT\_NEGATIVE\_BALANCE\_PROTECTION | 39  | การฝากเพื่อรีเซ็ตยอดคงเหลือติดลบเป็นศูนย์สำหรับบัญชีที่ได้รับการคุ้มครอง |
| BALANCE\_RESERVED1 | 40  | สงวนไว้สำหรับการใช้งานในอนาคต |
| BALANCE\_RESERVED2 | 41  | สงวนไว้สำหรับการใช้งานในอนาคต |
| BALANCE\_RESERVED3 | 42  | สงวนไว้สำหรับการใช้งานในอนาคต |

ProtoChangeBonusType [¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/th/references/public-server-model/#protochangebonustype "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ชื่อ | ค่า | คำอธิบาย |
| --- | --- | --- |
