# Mass Index - Knowledge base

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/mass-index/#mass-index)

Mass Index[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/mass-index/#mass-index "Permanent link")

======================================================================================================================

Definition[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/mass-index/#definition "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mass Index (MI) is a technical indicator that examines the fluctuations between high and low stock prices over a specific period. It suggests that a reversal of the current trend is likely when the range expands beyond a certain threshold and then subsequently narrows.

MobileDesktop

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/mass-index-ios.png)

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/mass-index.png)

[Open live account](https://ctrader.com/brokers)
 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

History[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/mass-index/#history "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mass Index was developed by Donald Dorsey in the early 1990s as a tool for identifying potential trend reversals in financial markets. It was first introduced in his book "The New Trading Dimensions" published in 1994, where he emphasised the importance of price volatility in market analysis.

Calculations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/mass-index/#calculations "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mass Index is calculated by first determining its [**Exponential Moving Average (EMA)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/exponential-moving-average/)
, followed by the Exponential Moving Average of this EMA (a double average). The ratio of these two averages is then summed over a specified number of periods.

\\\[ Mass\\ Index = \\sum\_{i=1}^n { {EMA\\ (High - Low)} \\over {EMA\\ (EMA\\ (High - Low))} } \\\]

\\(n\\) – the number of periods

\\((High - Low)\\) – the range for each period

\\(EMA\\) \\((High - Low)\\) – the EMA of the range

\\(EMA\\) \\((EMA\\) \\((High - Low))\\) – the EMA of the above

Interpretation[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/mass-index/#interpretation "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mass Index indicator is most commonly used with the 9-day period for the EMA and 25 days for the Mass Index calculation period. A Mass Index value below 26.5 typically suggests that the market is in a stable condition without significant volatility. In this range, there may not be an imminent trend reversal. When the Mass Index rises above a key threshold and then falls below it, it signals a possible trend reversal.

Application[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/mass-index/#application "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

While monitoring the Mass Index, the key trading signal is the so-called "reversal bulge". This occurs when the 25-period Mass Index rises above 27 and then falls back below 26.5. Such behaviour is interpreted as a probable signal of trend reversal (regardless of the trend direction).

*   **Buy signal** – after a reversal bulge in the Mass Index, if the price crosses above the 9-day EMA. This indicates that the price is gaining upward momentum, suggesting a buy opportunity, as the market may be entering an upward trend.
    
*   **Sell signal** – after a reversal bulge in the Mass Index, if the price crosses below the 9-day EMA. This signals that the price is weakening and could be entering a downtrend, suggesting a sell opportunity.
    
*   **Stop-loss placement** – placing a stop loss just below recent support levels or the moving average helps protect against losses if the market moves against a bullish signal from the Mass Index. Conversely, a stop loss is typically set just above recent resistance levels or the moving average to buffer against unexpected upward movements following a bearish signal from the Mass Index.
    
*   **Confirming trades** – traders often enhance their decision-making by using the MI alongside other technical indicators, such as the [**Simple Moving Average (SMA)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/)
    , to confirm potential reversal signals. Also, a confirmation from the [**Relative Strength Index (RSI)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/relative-strength-index/)
     indicating the oversold or overbought conditions can strengthen the signal derived from the Mass Index​.
    

Note

You can take advantage of algo trading, with cBots executing trades based on the signals from this indicator, as shown in our [**examples**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/references/Indicators/MassIndex/)
. Learn more about how to [**use indicators in cBots**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/)
.

Limitations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/mass-index/#limitations "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Since the Mass Index does not specify the direction of market movement, it is recommended to use it in conjunction with other indicators to confirm potential reversal signals (see application section). In strongly trending markets, the Mass Index may not provide valuable signals, as it focuses more on volatility rather than the direction of the trend​.

Summary[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/mass-index/#summary "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Mass Index indicator is designed to detect potential trend reversals by measuring the range between the high and low prices over time. It is a volatility-based indicator that focuses on the expansion and contraction of price ranges, without providing directional signals. Traders often look for specific patterns, such as a reversal bulge, where the index rises above a certain value (such as 27) and then falls back below a threshold (such as 26.5), signalling a potential reversal in the current price trend.

[Open live account](https://ctrader.com/brokers)

# Awesome Oscillator - Knowledge base

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/awesome-oscillator/#awesome-oscillator)

Awesome Oscillator[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/awesome-oscillator/#awesome-oscillator "Permanent link")

==============================================================================================================================================

Definition[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/awesome-oscillator/#definition "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Awesome Oscillator (AO) is a technical analysis tool designed to help traders assess market momentum and potential price reversals. A common utility for AO is to affirm the validity of trends perceived through price data. The AO is particularly useful for short-term trading strategies, such as day trading and scalping.

MobileDesktop

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/awesome-oscillator-ios.png)

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/awesome-oscillator.png)

[Open live account](https://ctrader.com/brokers)
 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

History[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/awesome-oscillator/#history "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Bill Williams developed the Awesome Oscillator and introduced it in his book "New Trading Dimensions", published in 1998. The AO builds upon the [**Accelerator Oscillator (AC)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/accelerator-oscillator/)
, an indicator Williams developed and introduced in his book "Trading Chaos", published in 1995.

Calculations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/awesome-oscillator/#calculations "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Awesome Oscillator considers the [**Simple Moving Average**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/)
 of two periods: 5-period and 34-period. It then subtracts the latter from the former.

\\\[ AO = { SMA\_5 - SMA\_{34} } \\\]

Interpretation[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/awesome-oscillator/#interpretation "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [**Median Prices**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/median-price/)
 are utilised in calculating the Simple Moving Averages for the AO indicator.

The main patterns of the indicator behaviour can be interpreted as follows:

*   **Zero-line crossovers** – the AO crossing above the zero line indicates potential upward momentum. The AO crossing below the zero line suggests potential downward momentum.
    
*   **Divergence** – when the price makes new highs while the AO makes lower highs, this can signal a potential trend reversal. When the price makes new lows while the AO makes higher lows, this may indicate a potential reversal to the upside.
    

Application[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/awesome-oscillator/#application "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Buy signal** – traders might consider entering a buy position when the AO crosses above the zero line, suggesting upward momentum. Confirmation from other tools like moving averages can help verify the signal.
    
*   **Sell signal** – a sell signal may occur when the AO crosses below the zero line, indicating potential downward momentum.
    
*   **Stop-loss placement** – traders might set a stop loss below significant lows when using the AO in bullish trends or above significant highs in bearish trends to manage risk.
    
*   **Exit strategies** – the AO can help confirm exit points when momentum weakens. If the AO begins to approach the zero line after a strong movement, it may indicate a loss of momentum, suggesting an opportunity to exit the trade.
    

Note

You can take advantage of algo trading, with cBots executing trades based on the signals from this indicator, as shown in our [**examples**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/references/Indicators/AwesomeOscillator/)
. Learn more about how to [**use indicators in cBots**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/)
.

Limitations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/awesome-oscillator/#limitations "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

While the AO helps assess momentum, it only measures historical data, which can result in delayed signals. Additionally, the AO does not provide exact entry or exit points and relying solely on it might lead to false signals in choppy markets.

Summary[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/awesome-oscillator/#summary "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Awesome Oscillator is a momentum indicator that helps traders assess market momentum by comparing the difference between two Simple Moving Averages of different periods. It oscillates above and below a zero line, indicating bullish or bearish momentum. The AO is particularly useful in identifying potential market reversals and can be effectively combined with other indicators, such as the [**Bollinger Bands**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/volatility/bollinger-bands/)
, to enhance trading decisions and confirm signals. When using the AO, traders should consider its limitations, such as false signals in choppy markets.

# Accelerator Oscillator - Knowledge base

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/accelerator-oscillator/#accelerator-oscillator)

Accelerator Oscillator[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/accelerator-oscillator/#accelerator-oscillator "Permanent link")

==========================================================================================================================================================

Definition[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/accelerator-oscillator/#definition "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Accelerator Oscillator (AC) is a technical analysis tool that tracks the speed of changes in price momentum. It shows whether the market driving force accelerates or decelerates, helping traders anticipate potential reversals. By identifying these momentum shifts early, traders can better time their entries and exits before price changes become apparent.

MobileDesktop

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/accelerator-oscillator-ios.png)

![](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/img/accelerator-oscillator.png)

[Open live account](https://ctrader.com/brokers)
 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

History[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/accelerator-oscillator/#history "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Bill Williams introduced the Accelerator Oscillator in his 1995 book "Trading Chaos" as part of his broader trading methodology. Williams later developed the [**Awesome Oscillator (AO)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/awesome-oscillator/)
 and introduced it in his book "New Trading Dimensions", published in 1998. The AO builds on data provided by the AC.

Calculations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/accelerator-oscillator/#calculations "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Accelerator Oscillator is calculated by subtracting a 5-period [**Simple Moving Average (SMA)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/trend/simple-moving-average/)
 of the Awesome Oscillator from the AO itself.

\\\[ AC = { AO - SMA\\ ( AO,\\ 5 ) } \\\]

\\(AO\\) – the Awesome Oscillator, which is the difference between a 34-period and 5-period SMA applied to the [**Median Price**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/other/median-price/)

\\(SMA\\) ( \\(AO\\), \\(5\\) ) – the 5-period Simple Moving Average of the Awesome Oscillator

Interpretation[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/accelerator-oscillator/#interpretation "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Zero-line crossovers** – a cross above zero suggests growing bullish momentum, while a cross below signals increasing bearish momentum.
    
*   **Growing bars** – consecutive rising bars above or below zero confirm the strength of an ongoing trend (uptrend or downtrend).
    

Application[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/accelerator-oscillator/#application "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Accelerator Oscillator can offer insights into potential buy and sell opportunities based on momentum shifts.

*   **Buy signal** – traders might consider entering a buy position when the AC crosses above the zero line, which could indicate increasing bullish momentum. Growing green bars may further suggest sustained upward momentum, potentially confirming the trade.
    
*   **Sell signal** – a sell position could be considered when the AC crosses below the zero line, signalling the possibility of increasing bearish momentum. Falling red bars might suggest continued downward pressure, offering additional confirmation.
    
*   **Stop-loss placement** – traders may choose to place a stop loss just below the zero line for buy positions or just above the zero line for sell positions, as a way to manage risk in case of a momentum reversal.
    
*   **Confirming trades** – to improve the reliability of signals, traders may opt to combine the AC with other indicators, such as moving averages, the [**Relative Strength Index (RSI)**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/relative-strength-index/)
     or the [**MACD**](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/macd-crossover/)
    , to help confirm the direction and strength of momentum before entering a position.
    

Note

You can take advantage of algo trading, with cBots executing trades based on the signals from this indicator, as shown in our [**examples**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/references/Indicators/AcceleratorOscillator/)
. Learn more about how to [**use indicators in cBots**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/)
.

Limitations[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/accelerator-oscillator/#limitations "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Accelerator Oscillator tends to produce false signals in choppy or range-bound markets. Additionally, it does not indicate trend direction. The indicator can also lag in highly volatile conditions.

Summary[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/knowledge-base/indicators/oscillators/accelerator-oscillator/#summary "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Accelerator Oscillator helps identify early momentum shifts to predict potential reversals. It signals bullish or bearish momentum based on its position relative to the zero line, calculated by subtracting a 5-period Simple Moving Average of the Awesome Oscillator from the AO itself. The AC is an effective and popular tool that helps identify accelerations or decelerations in the market driving force.

 [Download cTrader](https://ctrader.com/download/)

# How to use indicators in cBots - cTrader Algo

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/#how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots)

How to use indicators in cBots[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/#how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots "Permanent link")

=================================================================================================================================================================================

In this article and its corresponding video, we explain how you can use built-in indicators in your cBots. Using indicators in a cBot is typically done for a variety of purposes, most notably making cBots take trading decisions depending on indicator outputs. To demonstrate this, we will develop a simple [**automated trading strategy**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-traders/key-types-of-cbots/)
 based on the Relative Strength Index (RSI).

Create a new cBot[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/#create-a-new-cbot "Permanent link")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To create a new cBot, switch to the **Algo** tab and click **New cBot**.

We will rename our cBot to "RSI cBot". Here is how our automated trading strategy will function.

*   The bot will enter buy positions when the RSI indicator moves below a predefined threshold.
*   The bot will enter sell positions when the RSI indicator moves above a predefined threshold.

Define and initialise the indicator[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/#define-and-initialise-the-indicator "Permanent link")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The first thing we need to do is define the RSI indicator in our cBot class. To do so, we declare a new field.

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| 1   | `private RelativeStrengthIndicator rsi;` |

Using private class members

We can safely make the `rsi` field private as it will only be used in our cBot.

All cTrader indicators need to be initialised before we can use them. This is best done in the body of the `OnStart()` method so that the cBot has access to the indicator output after it begins running. cTrader offers a handy class that allows you to easily initialise built-in indicators using ready-made constructors. Before we can see how this is done, we will add two parameters necessary for initialising the RSI indicator.

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| 1<br>2<br>3<br>4<br>5 | `[Parameter("Source", Group = "RSI")] public DataSeries Source { get; set; }  [Parameter("Periods", Group = "RSI", DefaultValue = 14)] public int Periods { get; set; }` |

Now that we have added our parameters, we are ready to initialise the indicator.

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| 1<br>2<br>3<br>4 | `protected override void OnStart() {     rsi = Indicators.RelativeStrengthIndex(Source, Periods); }` |

Implement trading logic[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/#implement-trading-logic "Permanent link")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As we have already initialised the indicator, we can call it and use it in our trading logic. In the case of the RSI indicator, we can read the outputs using the `Results` collection.

To do so, we will use the `OnBar()` method and implement the following conditions.

*   If the current RSI value is below the RSI buy threshold, enter a new buy position.
*   If the current RSI value is above the RSI sell threshold, enter a new sell position.

Because we rely on predefined thresholds to execute our trading logic, we will add two more parameters to our cBot.

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| 1<br>2<br>3<br>4<br>5 | `[Parameter("Buy Level", Group = "RSI", DefaultValue = 30)] public int BuyLevel { get; set; }  [Parameter("Sell Level", Group = "RSI", DefaultValue = 70)] public int SellLevel { get; set; }` |

Next, we will implement our conditions in the cBot code.

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| 1<br> 2<br> 3<br> 4<br> 5<br> 6<br> 7<br> 8<br> 9<br>10<br>11 | `protected override void OnBarClosed() {     if (rsi.Result.LastValue < BuyLevel)     {     // Enter buy position     }     else if (rsi.Result.LastValue > SellLevel)     {     // Enter sell position     } }` |

It is time to implement auxiliary methods for executing trading orders. This is done to create an additional level of abstraction in the code, improving readability and reusability.

We start by adding one more cBot parameter that allows you to set the traded volume.

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| 1<br>2 | `[Parameter("Quantity (Lots)", Group = "Volume", DefaultValue = 1, MinValue = 0.01, Step = 0.01)] public double Quantity { get; set; }` |

Afterwards, we will add the `Open()` and `Close()` methods for entering a new position and closing all positions opened by the cBot in a given direction.

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| 1<br> 2<br> 3<br> 4<br> 5<br> 6<br> 7<br> 8<br> 9<br>10<br>11<br>12<br>13<br>14 | `private void Open(TradeType tradeType) {     var position = Positions.Find("SampleRSI", SymbolName, tradeType);     var volumeInUnits = Symbol.QuantityToVolumeInUnits(Quantity);      if (position == null)         ExecuteMarketOrder(tradeType, SymbolName, volumeInUnits, "RSI cBot"); }  private void Close(TradeType tradeType) {     foreach (var position in Positions.FindAll("SampleRSI", SymbolName, tradeType))         ClosePosition(position); }` |

We can use our auxiliary methods to complete the automated trading strategy.

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| 1<br> 2<br> 3<br> 4<br> 5<br> 6<br> 7<br> 8<br> 9<br>10<br>11<br>12<br>13 | `protected override void OnTick() {     if (rsi.Result.LastValue < BuyLevel)     {         Close(TradeType.Sell);         Open(TradeType.Buy);     }     else if (rsi.Result.LastValue > SellLevel)     {         Close(TradeType.Buy);         Open(TradeType.Sell);     } }` |

Backtest the cBot[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-developers/how-to-use-indicators-in-cbots/#backtest-the-cbot "Permanent link")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

After we finish coding the cBot, we can build it and switch to the **Backtesting** tab to see how it performs on historical data. [**Click here**](https://help.ctrader.com/ctrader-algo/articles/for-traders/how-to-backtest-a-cbot/)
 to learn more about backtesting.

We can also attach the RSI indicator to the current chart to see whether the trades executed by the cBot match the intended entry points.

[Subscribe to our YouTube channel](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCgoyyb_r8sgNrf1nqyMyo5A?sub_confirmation=1)

Back to top

# Create WV plugins with AI - WebView Plugins

 

[Skip to content](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/how-tos/create-wv-plugins-with-ai/#create-webview-plugins-with-ai)

Warning

This feature is in pre-release and currently under testing. Documentation is for reference only.

Create WebView plugins with AI[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/how-tos/create-wv-plugins-with-ai/#create-webview-plugins-with-ai "Permanent link")

===============================================================================================================================================================

In just a few steps, you can easily create a web service for your WebView plugin with AI. No programming knowledge or skills are required.

Brainstorm plugin ideas[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/how-tos/create-wv-plugins-with-ai/#brainstorm-plugin-ideas "Permanent link")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you already know what type of web service you want to create, skip this section and move on to the next.

Alternatively, you can ask your preferred AI to brainstorm ideas.

Prompt

`I want to create a web app that will be displayed as a WebView plugin within cTrader.  Suggest useful ideas that will enhance my trading experience.`

Answer

*   Smart trade assistant
*   Trade calculator
*   Custom symbol dashboard
*   Symbol notes & collaboration tool
*   Risk management dashboard
*   News impact tracker
*   Session heatmap tool
*   Trade co-pilot (AI notes & feedback)

Generate the website code[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/how-tos/create-wv-plugins-with-ai/#generate-the-website-code "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

WebView plugins in cTrader require externally hosted websites that use the standard stack of JavaScript, HTML, CSS and (if necessary) WebView SDK methods.

For a more accurate response, provide the AI with the following input data:

*   [**General information**](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/)
     about WebView plugins
*   [**SDK references**](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/references/public-server-message/)
     for WebView plugins
*   cTrader Store [**terms of use**](https://ctrader.com/terms-of-use)
    
*   App Store and Google Play rules

Warning

Any WebView plugin you create must comply with the rules of cTrader Store, App Store and Google Play. Most importantly, your web service must not include authentication or payment forms.

For your convenience, you can [**download**](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/assets/introduction-and-references.zip)
 the WebView plugin documentation and cTrader Store rules, in case you prefer to upload them as files for the AI to reference.

Prompt

`I want to create a trade calculator web app to be used as a WebView plugin in cTrader.  The website should be built using only JavaScript, HTML, CSS and the SDK methods for WebView plugins.  No additional libraries should be required.  The website must comply with the rules of cTrader Store, Apple's App Store and Google’s Play Store. The website must not include authentication or payment forms. Provide a single piece of code that contains everything necessary to build the website without any additional setup.`

Answer

``<!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head>   <meta charset="UTF-8" />   <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"/>   <title>Trade Calculator</title>   <style>     body {       font-family: Arial, sans-serif;       margin: 20px;       background-color: #f4f4f4;       color: #333;     }     h1 {       text-align: center;     }     .container {       max-width: 400px;       margin: auto;       padding: 20px;       background: white;       border-radius: 10px;       box-shadow: 0 0 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);     }     label, input, select {       display: block;       width: 100%;       margin-bottom: 15px;     }     input, select {       padding: 8px;       font-size: 1em;     }     button {       padding: 10px;       background-color: #007acc;       color: white;       border: none;       font-size: 1em;       cursor: pointer;       width: 100%;     }     button:hover {       background-color: #005fa3;     }     .result {       margin-top: 20px;       font-weight: bold;     }   </style> </head> <body>   <div class="container">     <h1>Trade Calculator</h1>     <label for="symbol">Symbol (e.g., EURUSD)</label>     <input type="text" id="symbol" value="EURUSD"/>      <label for="volume">Volume (Lots)</label>     <input type="number" id="volume" value="1" min="0.01" step="0.01"/>      <label for="price">Price</label>     <input type="number" id="price" value="1.1000" step="0.0001"/>      <label for="leverage">Leverage</label>     <input type="number" id="leverage" value="100" step="1"/>      <button onclick="calculate()">Calculate</button>      <div class="result" id="result">Margin: --</div>   </div>    <script>     function calculate() {       const volume = parseFloat(document.getElementById("volume").value);       const price = parseFloat(document.getElementById("price").value);       const leverage = parseFloat(document.getElementById("leverage").value);        // Standard 100,000 units per lot       const notional = 100000 * volume;       const marginRequired = (notional / leverage) * price;        document.getElementById("result").innerText =          `Margin Required: ${marginRequired.toFixed(2)} (base currency units)`;     }      // WebView Plugin SDK Example     window.addEventListener("message", (event) => {       const message = event.data;       if (message.type === "symbolUpdate") {         document.getElementById("symbol").value = message.symbol;       }     });      // Send initial readiness signal     window.parent.postMessage({ type: "ready" }, "*");   </script> </body> </html>``

Deploy the web service[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/how-tos/create-wv-plugins-with-ai/#deploy-the-web-service "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Paste the generated code into an HTML file and save it (locally) on your computer.

To deploy the web service, you need to host the HTML file on a secure server. You have to acquire a domain name, register with a hosting provider and then upload your HTML file using cPanel/hPanel or via FTP.

You can ask the AI for assistance using these prompts:

Prompt

`Walk me through getting a domain name and setting it up with a hosting provider.`

Test the web app[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/how-tos/create-wv-plugins-with-ai/#test-the-web-app "Permanent link")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Open the new website in your browser. Test the features on the site to confirm that they work properly.

![](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/img/how-tos/trade-calculator-example.jpeg)

Depending on the appearance of your live website, you might want to return to your AI tool and ask it for refinements. For example, you can ask the AI to edit the website code as follows:

*   Fix a functionality that is not working properly
*   Change the colour theme
*   Add a specific feature
*   Remove a specific feature
*   And more

After making the adjustments, return to the file, paste the edited code and deploy the changes.

Once your web app is ready and you know the URL, you can proceed to [**build your WebView plugin**](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/how-tos/build-wv-plugins/)
 in cTrader.

# Sdk vs open api - Plugins de WebView

 

[Saltar a contenido](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/wv-plugin-sdk/sdk-vs-open-api/#webview-plugin-sdk-vs-open-api)

Advertencia

This feature is in pre-release and currently under testing. Documentation is for reference only.

WebView plugin SDK vs Open API[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/wv-plugin-sdk/sdk-vs-open-api/#webview-plugin-sdk-vs-open-api "Permanent link")

==============================================================================================================================================================

When building web apps for WebView plugins, developers can use the [**WebView plugin SDK**](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/how-tos/use-wv-plugins-sdk/)
, [**Open API**](https://help.ctrader.com/open-api/)
 or both to enable communication between their web services and the cTrader platform. This guide will help you understand when to use each.

Overview[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/wv-plugin-sdk/sdk-vs-open-api/#overview "Permanent link")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Both the WV plugin SDK and Open API offer powerful capabilities, but they serve different purposes in terms of scope, trust model and permitted operations.

| **Attribute** | **WV plugin SDK** | **Open API** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Context of use | Internal and embedded in cTrader.  <br>Used in web apps for WebView plugins. | External and public, not tied to any specific flow.  <br>Can be used by any service. |
| Authentication | Automatically authenticated inside cTrader. | Requires additional authentication or OAuth 2.0 tokens. |
| Permissions | Full capabilities including for sensitive operations. | Limited to non-sensitive data. |
| Performance | Excessive request volumes may affect the plugin. | Scales for large data queries and aggregation. |

Shared capabilities[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/wv-plugin-sdk/sdk-vs-open-api/#shared-capabilities "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

For WebView plugins, some functionalities are available through both the [**WV plugin SDK**](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/references/public-server-message/)
 and [**Open API**](https://help.ctrader.com/open-api/messages/)
. You can use either to do the following:

*   Get historical data
*   Get symbol information
*   Subscribe to quotes
*   Retrieve account data (balance and equity).

WV plugin SDKOpen API

Implementing account data retrieval using the [**WV plugin SDK**](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/references/public-server-message/)
 means seamless operations:

*   No token required from the user.
    
*   No separate login flow needed.
    
*   Authentication is handled automatically by cTrader.
    

When your web application runs inside cTrader as a WebView plugin, it inherits the user's authenticated session. This setup makes account data retrieval faster, safer and more user-friendly.

Note

The WV plugin SDK enables developers to create web apps for WebView plugins entirely with frontend technologies (JavaScript, HTML and CSS), or [**create WV plugins with AI**](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/how-tos/create-wv-plugins-with-ai/)
. You would not have to implement or maintain a backend service.

Implementing account data retrieval using [**Open API**](https://help.ctrader.com/open-api/messages/)
 in your web application means one or more of the following:

*   You must implement an OAuth 2.0 flow in your application.
    
*   Traders must log in to cTrader and authorise your app.
    
*   Traders must generate and provide a personal access token.
    

Warning

While Open API also supports the retrieval of certain [**account data**](https://help.ctrader.com/open-api/model-messages/#protooatrader)
, it comes with limitations for WV plugin developers. The extra authentication step complicates development, disrupts the user experience and may discourage adoption of your product.

Key differences[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/wv-plugin-sdk/sdk-vs-open-api/#key-differences "Permanent link")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Web apps can perform various trading actions in cTrader using the WV plugin SDK, whereas Open API does not support these functionalities for WebView plugins.

| **WV plugin operation** | **WV plugin SDK** | **Open API** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Create new orders | Yes | No  |
| Modify existing orders | Yes | No  |
| Cancel orders | Yes | No  |
| Close positions | Yes | No  |
| Modify SL/TP protection | Yes | No  |
| Navigate screens | Yes | No  |

Advertencia

WebView plugins operate without accessing or transferring any personal user information, such as user names or account identifiers. While plugins can access limited account data (e.g., balance) when actively connected in the cTrader environment, they do not store cookies at any point.

cTrader limits sensitive operations to the platform environment, where:

*   The user has explicitly installed or enabled the plugin.
    
*   The platform can enforce extra confirmation flows.
    
*   Position limits, margin checks and risk rules run locally before the request is sent.
    

An external website using Open API cannot guarantee the same level of control and security; therefore, trade-related endpoints cannot be exposed there.

Summary[¶](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/wv-plugin-sdk/sdk-vs-open-api/#summary "Permanent link")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Use the WV plugin SDK in web apps that will be used to [**build a WebView plugin**](https://help.ctrader.com/webview-plugins/es/how-tos/build-wv-plugins/)
, especially if the web app needs to trade or deal with certain account data.

Use Open API in web apps that run outside cTrader and rely on public data.

Volver al principio
